questions.promptTex: \text{Which equation describes a line with slope } 4 \\ \text{passing through } (2, 5)?
hints: $y_1$ is the y-coordinate of the point — it goes with $y$. $x_1$ is the x-coordinate — it goes with $x$.
Distribute the $2$ on the right side first.
Then add $3$ to both sides to isolate $y$.

steps.text: **Point-slope → slope-intercept conversion: ALWAYS distribute first, then move the constant.**

questions.promptTex: \text{A line through } (3, 7) \text{ has slope } -2. \\ \text{What is its y-intercept?}
hints: Convert to slope-intercept by distributing and adding $7$.

steps.text: **Strategy: write the line in point-slope form, then convert to slope-intercept to read off $b$.**

questions.promptTex: \text{A line passes through } (-2, 4) \text{ and } (1, -2). \\ \text{Which is its point-slope equation using } (1, -2)?

steps.text: **Two-step recipe.** (1) Find the slope from two points. (2) Plug into point-slope using EITHER point.
**Simplify the double-negative on the LEFT.** $y - (-2) = y + 2$. Final form: $y + 2 = -2(x - 1)$.
**Tip on the double-negative.** $y - y_1$ becomes $y + |y_1|$ whenever $y_1$ is negative. Same trick on the right with $x - x_1$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which point lies on the line } y - 4 = 3(x + 2)?
hints: The form is $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$.
Read off $(x_1, y_1)$ — but watch the signs! $x + 2 = x - (-2)$.
questions.explanation: Rewrite as $y - 4 = 3(x - (-2))$. So $(x_1, y_1) = (-2, 4)$ — that point is on the line.

questions.promptTex: \text{Write the point-slope form of a line through } (3, -1) \text{ with slope } 5.
questions.explanation: $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ with point $(3, -1)$ and slope 5: $y - (-1) = 5(x - 3) \Rightarrow y + 1 = 5(x - 3)$.
steps: **Use the point-slope template.** $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$.
**Common mistake.** Forgetting to flip $-(-1)$ to $+1$ — the negative sign in the point makes the form sign-tricky. Always rewrite to remove the double negative.
**Distribute carefully — watch the sign across $(x + 3)$.** $-2 \cdot x + (-2) \cdot 3 = -2x - 6$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the y-intercept of the line } y - 7 = 3(x - 2).
hints: Convert to slope-intercept first, OR plug in $x = 0$.
steps: **Two equally valid methods. Pick whichever is faster.**
**Method B: convert to slope-intercept.** Distribute: $y - 7 = 3x - 6$. Add 7: $y = 3x + 1$. Read off $b = 1$.
**Both give the same answer.** Method A is faster when you just need the y-intercept. Method B is better when you also need the equation for other purposes.

questions.promptTex: \text{Write the equation through } (-2, 5) \text{ and } (4, -1) \text{ in point-slope form} \\ \text{using the point } (4, -1).

hints: Look for the form (y - number) = (number)(x ± number).
questions.explanation: $y - 3 = 4(x + 1)$ matches the point-slope template $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ with point $(-1, 3)$ and $m = 4$.
steps: **Recognize the THREE standard forms of a line.**
**Classify each option.** $y = 2x + 5$ → slope-intercept. $y - 3 = 4(x+1)$ → POINT-SLOPE ✓. $2x + 3y = 6$ → standard. $y = -x$ → slope-intercept (with $b = 0$).

**Why know all three forms.** Each is convenient for different tasks. Slope-intercept: read off slope and y-intercept. Point-slope: build from a point and a slope. Standard: integer coefficients, useful for systems of equations.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the slope of the line described by } y + 2 = -\dfrac{3}{4}(x - 6)?
hints: Point-slope: $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$. $m$ is the multiplier on $(x - x_1)$.
questions.explanation: In $y + 2 = -\dfrac{3}{4}(x - 6)$, the slope is the coefficient on $(x - x_1)$: $m = -\dfrac{3}{4}$.
steps: **Read off the slope directly — it's the COEFFICIENT of $(x - x_1)$.**
**Match to template.** $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$. The number multiplying $(x - x_1)$ on the right is $m$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Write the line with slope } 2 \text{ through } (0, -3) \text{ in point-slope form.}
hints: Plug into $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ with $(0, -3)$ and $m = 2$.
steps: **Interesting.** Since the point is on the y-axis ($x = 0$), this is also slope-intercept form: $y + 3 = 2x \Rightarrow y = 2x - 3$. Both forms describe the same line.
